questions.question: Data loss (or data exfiltration) is defined in the lecture as when data is:
questions.option_a: A. Compressed to save storage space.
questions.option_b: B. Intentionally or unintentionally lost, stolen, or leaked to the outside world.
questions.option_c: C. Encrypted with a strong cipher algorithm.
questions.option_d: D. Backed up to an internal server.

questions.question: According to the lecture, data is considered to be an organization's:
Which of the following is explicitly listed as an example of organizational data?
Which item is NOT listed as organizational data in the lecture?
A potential consequence of data loss mentioned in the lecture is:
questions.option_b: B. Brand damage and loss of reputation.

questions.question: Data loss can result in loss of competitive advantage and also:
Which of the following is mentioned as a possible impact of a breach related to data loss?
questions.option_a: A. Litigation/legal action resulting in fines and civil penalties.
questions.option_b: B. Free security upgrades from vendors.
questions.option_d: D. No need to notify anyone.

questions.question: The lecture notes that data loss can involve significant cost and effort to:
questions.option_a: A. Notify affected parties and recover from the breach.
questions.option_b: B. Rewrite all software from scratch.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a vector of data loss?
Which set contains only vectors of data loss listed in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Email, social networking, removable media.

questions.question: “Improper access control” is categorized in the lecture as a:
What does the acronym BYOD stand for?
BYOD is described as employees using their personal devices to:
questions.option_b: B. Remotely access organizational networks for office work.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example activity enabled by BYOD mentioned in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Access official email on a smartphone.
questions.option_d: D. Use only desktop computers at the office.

questions.question: A benefit of BYOD mentioned in the lecture is that it:
questions.option_b: B. Boosts productivity because employees can work anytime.

questions.question: BYOD may make employees happier because they:
questions.option_b: B. Use devices they are comfortable with.
questions.option_c: C. Have no access to email.

questions.question: A financial benefit of BYOD stated in the lecture is:
questions.option_a: A. Companies invest more in buying hardware.
questions.option_b: B. Reduced investment in buying hardware/software since employees use their own devices.
questions.option_c: C. Mandatory purchase of new tablets for all staff.

questions.question: BYOD helps companies stay abreast of changing technology because:
questions.option_a: A. Employees’ personal devices tend to stay up-to-date.
questions.option_b: B. It prevents any device upgrades.
questions.option_c: C. It forces everyone to use old devices.

questions.question: A major drawback of BYOD is that security threats arise due to:
questions.option_b: B. More devices accessing company data and possible malware entry from unsecured devices.

questions.question: Another risk of BYOD described in the lecture is that company data could:
questions.option_a: A. Never be accessed by employees.
questions.option_b: B. End up in wrong hands and be seen or stolen by outsiders.

questions.question: BYOD devices being stolen or lost can lead to:
The lecture notes IT departments face tremendous pressure in BYOD environments for:
questions.option_a: A. Support, managing, and securing all BYOD devices.

questions.question: What does the acronym COPE stand for?
COPE is a business model in which an organization:
questions.option_b: B. Provides employees with mobile devices and allows personal-style use.

questions.question: Compared to BYOD, COPE provides the organization with greater power to protect data:
questions.option_d: D. Only by using older devices.

questions.question: A listed benefit of COPE is the ability to actively manage and control:
questions.option_b: B. If/when a device can access particular apps, sites, services, networks, and solutions.

questions.question: COPE policies offer the opportunity to ______ a device of corporate data when an employee loses it.
COPE can incorporate controls that determine:
questions.option_a: A. How applications/networks/IT systems can be used remotely and whether information can be retrieved in certain scenarios.
questions.option_b: B. Only the color theme of apps.

questions.question: Mobile Device Management (MDM) is described as providing features to:
questions.option_a: A. Secure, monitor, and manage mobile devices.
questions.option_b: B. Replace encryption with plain text.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as an MDM security measure for COPE/BYOD?
PIN enforcement and strong authentication mechanisms are mentioned as:
questions.option_c: C. Vectors of data loss only.

questions.question: Remote data wipe helps address which scenario?
DLP options in MDM are intended to support:
Jailbreak/root detection is included in the lecture as part of:
Which is also listed as an MDM capability?
questions.option_b: B. Generating SHA-512 digests for routers.
questions.option_d: D. Blocking all social networks globally.

questions.question: Security assessments listed for COPE/BYOD include:
questions.option_a: A. Vulnerability assessments, penetration testing, audits.

questions.question: In the lecture, the term “hacker” can mean all EXCEPT:
questions.option_a: A. A clever programmer improving programs.
questions.option_b: B. A network professional strengthening networks.
questions.option_c: C. A person who always follows all laws and never touches computers.
questions.option_d: D. A person trying to gain unauthorized access to devices on the Internet.

questions.question: One meaning of “hacker” in the lecture is:
The lecture includes hackers as individuals who run programs to:
questions.option_a: A. Prevent or slow network access to many users, or corrupt/wipe data on servers.

questions.question: White hat hackers are best described as:
questions.option_b: B. Ethical hackers using skills for good, ethical, and legal purposes.

questions.question: White hat hackers may perform which activities?
questions.option_a: A. Security assessments such as vulnerability assessment and penetration tests.
questions.option_b: B. Only DDoS attacks for attention.
questions.option_c: C. Only data exfiltration for money.

questions.question: White hats typically report discovered vulnerabilities to:
questions.option_b: B. Developers so they can be fixed before exploitation.
questions.option_c: C. Random social media accounts only.

questions.question: Some organizations may award white hat hackers:
questions.option_a: A. Prizes or bounties for reported vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Gray hat hackers (as defined in the lecture) typically:
questions.option_a: A. Commit crimes/unethical acts but not for personal gain or serious damage.
questions.option_b: B. Always work for government agencies.

questions.question: A gray hat example in the lecture is someone who:
questions.option_a: A. Compromises a system without permission then publicly discloses the vulnerability.
questions.option_b: B. Encrypts files with user consent.
questions.option_c: C. Conducts a paid audit with a contract.

questions.question: A risk of publicizing a vulnerability (gray hat behavior) is that it may:
questions.option_b: B. Give other hackers the opportunity to exploit it.
questions.option_d: D. Eliminate all data loss vectors.

questions.question: Black hat hackers are:
questions.option_b: B. Unethical criminals who violate security for personal gain or malicious reasons.

questions.question: Black hat hackers typically:
questions.option_a: A. Exploit vulnerabilities to compromise systems.
questions.option_d: D. Only monitor logs for compliance.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a modern hacking title in the lecture?
Script kiddies are described as:
questions.option_b: B. Inexperienced hackers running existing tools/exploits developed by others.

questions.question: The objective of many script kiddies is to:
questions.option_a: A. Impress friends or gain credit in enthusiast communities.

questions.question: The term “script kiddie” does NOT relate to the actual:
Vulnerability brokers are usually:
questions.option_b: B. Gray hat hackers who discover exploits and report them to vendors, sometimes for rewards.

questions.question: Cyber criminals are best described in the lecture as:
questions.option_a: A. Black hat hackers motivated to make money using any means necessary.
questions.option_c: C. Students practicing in labs only.
questions.option_d: D. People who never trade tools.

questions.question: Cyber criminals often operate in an underground economy buying/selling:
questions.option_a: A. Attack toolkits, zero-day exploit code, botnet services, banking Trojans, keyloggers.

questions.question: Cyber criminals target:
questions.option_b: B. Small businesses and consumers as well as large enterprises and industry verticals.

questions.question: Hacktivists are described as:
questions.option_a: A. Gray hat hackers who protest social/political ideas.

questions.question: Hacktivists primarily hack for:
Hacktivist activities mentioned include leaking sensitive information and performing:
questions.option_a: A. Distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks.
questions.option_b: B. Only data encryption at rest.

questions.question: Which group is listed as an example of hacktivists?
State-sponsored hackers are described as:
State-sponsored hackers often use:
State-sponsored hacking targets can include:
questions.option_a: A. Foreign governments, terrorist groups, and corporations.

questions.question: The lecture gives which example of state-sponsored malware created to damage Iran’s nuclear enrichment capabilities?
